1_Master_Workout_Split_Table.md: **Structure (3-day example):**
Day A: Squat pattern, Horizontal push, Horizontal pull, Core
Day B: Hinge pattern, Vertical push, Vertical pull, Arms
Day C: Lunge/unilateral, Push variation, Pull variation, Core
**Best for:** Never trained – 1 year; fat loss phases (frequency keeps strength up while calories are down); anyone with <4 days/week.
**Exclude when:** Client has >2 years training age AND wants specialization/bodybuilding aesthetics (frequency demands become harder to service per-muscle volume without excessive session length).
**Modification rules:** Add a 4th day (Full Body D) once weekly sets per muscle exceed what 3 sessions can hold (~10+ sets/muscle/week becomes hard to fit in 3 sessions without >20 sets/session).
**Progression:** Linear load progression session-to-session (beginners can progress every session on major lifts).

**Male/Female:** No structural difference; female clients often tolerate slightly higher frequency due to faster recovery from muscular fatigue (research trend, not absolute) — can move to 4x/week sooner.
**Younger trainees (teen):** Emphasize technique days 1–8 weeks before adding real load progression.
**Older trainees (50+):** Add 10 min dedicated joint-prep before Day A/B/C; prioritize machines over free-weight complexity in first 3 months.
**Home gym minimal equipment version:** Goblet squat, DB RDL, DB bench/floor press, band or DB row, plank variations.
**Full commercial gym version:** Barbell squat, barbell RDL, barbell bench, cable row, leg press, lat pulldown.
**Structure:** Upper A, Lower A, Upper B, Lower B (rest day between or after day 2).
**Best for:** 6 months–5 years training age; strength + hypertrophy blends; time-constrained lifters wanting quality frequency.
**Exclude when:** <4 days/week available; goal is pure specialization of a single muscle needing >20 sets/week (harder to fit without long sessions).
**Progression:** Double progression (rep range + load) once out of true beginner linear phase.
**Note on emphasis:** Upper A can be push-emphasis, Upper B pull-emphasis (or vice versa) to balance push:pull ratio at 1:1 to 1:1.5 for shoulder health.
**3-day version:** 1x frequency/muscle — best for time-constrained intermediates or advanced lifters using very high per-session volume.
**6-day version:** 2x frequency/muscle — gold standard for intermediate-advanced bodybuilders; allows MAV-level weekly volume without overloading single sessions.
**Exclude when:** <1 year training age (frequency undershoots ideal for beginners on the 3-day version; 6-day version is a large recovery demand best reserved once work capacity is built).
**Fatigue management:** On 6-day PPL, rotate primary compound emphasis (e.g., Push 1 = bench-focus, Push 2 = OHP-focus) to avoid CNS staleness from identical heavy lifts twice in 7 days.
**Structure:** Push, Pull, Legs, Upper, Lower — allows near-2x frequency without full 6-day commitment.
**Best for:** 2–10 years training age wanting bodybuilding results on 5 days.
**Rotation logic:** Rotate which day starts the week so no single day always follows the same fatigue pattern.
**Structure:** Chest, Back, Shoulders, Legs, Arms (or Legs split into Quad/Ham days).

**Best for:** 5+ years training age, already meeting MAV via other splits, wanting session-level muscle specialization and high per-session volume/pump-focused work with drop sets/mechanical drop sets.

**Exclude when:** Training age <5 years, goal = strength/powerlifting, or client cannot recover between single weekly muscle exposures (frequency of 1x/week is at the lower end of research-supported ranges — MUST be compensated with high per-session volume and excellent recovery).
**Coach note:** This is the most commonly *mis-assigned* split online. It is copied from advanced bodybuilders (often enhanced) without matching volume tolerance or recovery capacity.
**Structure:** Squat day, Bench day, Deadlift day, 4th day = weak-point accessory/secondary bench or squat.
**Best for:** Strength/powerlifting goals, 1+ year training age with mastered technique.
**Progression model:** Block periodization (Hypertrophy block → Strength block → Peaking block) or linear progression for novices.
**Structure:** Max Effort Upper, Max Effort Lower, Dynamic Effort Upper, Dynamic Effort Lower.
**Best for:** Advanced strength athletes (3+ years) who have plateaued on linear/block models.
**Exclude when:** Client cannot yet perform competent max-effort singles/triples safely (technique risk).
**Structure:** Day A (squat, push, pull, hinge accessory), Day B (hinge, push, pull, squat accessory) — every major pattern touched both days.
**Best for:** Maintenance phases, extremely busy professionals, return-from-injury ramp-up.

**Reality check for the AI to give clients:** 2 days/week can maintain muscle and can even build muscle in true beginners, but it is a floor, not an optimization strategy — set expectations accordingly.
**Structure:** Varies by sport and periodization phase (off-season = hypertrophy/strength bias; in-season = maintenance + power).
**Key rule:** In-season lifting volume drops 40–60% from off-season peak; frequency can stay similar, intensity/volume must drop.
Assigning 6-day PPL to someone under 1 year of training — frequency without adequate recovery capacity leads to accumulating fatigue and stalled progress.
Copying pro bodybuilders' bro splits without matching volume/recovery/pharmacological context.
Treating "more days" as always superior — a well-programmed 3-day Full Body outperforms a poorly-recovered 6-day split.
Never adjusting push:pull ratio, leading to shoulder impingement from press-dominant programming.
"You must train each muscle 1x/week for it to grow." — False; 2x/week generally outperforms 1x/week at equal volume for most trainees.
"More days always = more muscle." — False beyond the point where recovery capacity is exceeded.
"Full body splits are only for beginners." — False; well-designed full body templates work at any training age, especially in fat-loss phases.
2_Programming_Rules.md: **Who:** Never trained – 6 months.
**How:** Add load every session (or every week) on main lifts while reps/sets held constant.
**Example:** Squat 3x5 @ 60kg → 62.5kg → 65kg weekly, until failure to add reps at prescribed RIR.
**End condition:** Two consecutive failed attempts to progress → move to double progression.
**Who:** 6 months–5 years (primary model for most hypertrophy training).
**How:** Fix a rep range (e.g., 8–12). Add reps each session within the range at constant load. Once top of range is hit for all sets, increase load and drop back to bottom of range.
**Example:** Bench 3x8 @ 60kg → 3x9 → 3x10 → 3x11 → 3x12 → increase to 62.5kg, back to 3x8.
**Who:** Intermediate–advanced; those plateauing on linear/double progression; those needing variety to manage fatigue.
**How:** Vary rep range/intensity by session within the week (e.g., Mon = 5RM day, Wed = 10RM day, Fri = 15RM day for the same lift).
**Best for:** Breaking plateaus, powerbuilding hybrids.
**Who:** Advanced, especially strength/powerlifting.
**Structure:** Accumulation block (high volume, moderate intensity) → Intensification block (lower volume, higher intensity) → Realization/Peak block (very low volume, near-max intensity) → Deload.
**Typical block length:** 3–6 weeks each.
**Who:** Advanced strength athletes.

**How:** Train multiple qualities (max strength, speed-strength, hypertrophy) concurrently across the week rather than in sequential blocks; rotate exercise variations weekly (max effort) to manage the SAID principle and avoid accommodation.
**Who:** Any training age with sufficient body-awareness (usually 6+ months).
**How:** Prescribe a target RIR rather than fixed load; load is chosen by the client/AI feedback loop to hit that RIR on the day, adjusting for daily readiness (sleep, stress, soreness).
Female clients often show better recovery between sessions (research trend) — can tolerate slightly higher frequency/weekly volume at the higher end of ranges sooner in their training career.

Absolute strength standards differ, but relative programming rules (RIR, tempo, progression models) are identical — do not create a separate rulebook by sex beyond acknowledging typical recovery/volume tolerance trends and individual variation always overrides group trends.

Menstrual cycle: some clients report reduced RPE tolerance and strength in the late luteal/early follicular (pre-menstrual) phase — autoregulate intensity down slightly if the client reports this pattern; no blanket prescription should be applied without the client's own reported experience.
Chasing failure on every set of every exercise — leads to excess fatigue without proportional gains.
Never changing rep ranges/tempo for 6+ months, causing staleness and joint wear.
Ignoring RIR entirely and always going by a rigid percentage, which doesn't account for daily readiness.
Applying advanced periodization models (conjugate, block peaking) to beginners who need simple linear progression.
"You must always train to failure to grow." — False.
"RPE/RIR is unreliable so just lift as heavy as possible." — False; RIR accuracy improves with practice and is more useful than ignoring fatigue entirely.
"Slow tempo always builds more muscle." — False; tempo is a tool, not automatically superior.
3_Beginner_Programming.md: **Model:** Linear progression on all main lifts.
**Rule:** Add the smallest available load increment (2.5kg upper body, 5kg lower body, or use micro-plates) each session where the prescribed reps/sets were completed at target RIR.

**Failure-to-progress rule:** If a lift fails to progress for 2 consecutive sessions at the same weight/reps, switch that lift to double progression (fix load, add reps 1 at a time across a range, e.g. 8–12, then increase load).
**Deload rule for beginners:** Typically needed only every 8–12 weeks, or when 3+ lifts stall simultaneously combined with fatigue signs (see 10_Recovery_and_Deload.txt).
Soreness (DOMS) is common and expected in weeks 1–4; it is NOT a required signal of an effective workout and should decline as the body adapts.
Sleep target: 7–9 hours; beginners recover fast in absolute terms but poor sleep still blunts strength gains and increases injury risk from technique breakdown.
Deload frequency: every 8–12 weeks, or as needed (see file 10).
**Never trained + overweight clients:** Bias toward machines and supported positions initially (reduces balance demand, joint stress); build to free weights as competency and confidence grow.

**Younger trainees (teen):** Focus entirely on technique for first 8+ weeks; avoid heavy loading or failure; growth plates are not a contraindication to resistance training per current pediatric exercise science consensus, but coaching quality and supervision matter more.

**Older trainees (50+) who are also beginners:** Start with machines and supported movements, longer warm-ups, more conservative load jumps, and prioritize balance/mobility work alongside strength work.

**Returning after long layoff:** Programmed like a beginner for the first 2-4 weeks regardless of prior history, then can often re-progress faster ("muscle memory") — see 5_Advanced_Programming for detrained-advanced client protocols.
Overloading a true beginner with 5-6 days/week programming.
Starting with barbell back squat/deadlift on day one without any movement competency assessment.
Prescribing advanced volume (12-20 sets/muscle) from day one.
Chasing soreness as a proxy for effectiveness.
"Beginners need the most complex program to make sure nothing is missed." — False; simple, consistent programs outperform complex ones at this stage.
"You can't build real muscle without free weights." — False; DBs, bands, and machines are fully capable of driving beginner hypertrophy.
"No pain, no gain — you must be sore to know it worked." — False and counterproductive framing.
4_Intermediate_Programming.md: Fix rep range (e.g., 8-12), add reps within range across sessions, then increase load and reset to bottom of range.
Applies to nearly all accessory/isolation work and most compounds outside dedicated strength blocks.
Vary intensity/rep range by session across the week for the same lift.
Example (Bench Press 3x/week):
Best for breaking through plateaus and managing fatigue across a week.
Block 1 (Volume Accumulation): Higher sets, moderate intensity, RIR 2-3.
Block 2 (Intensification): Lower sets, higher intensity, RIR 1-2.
Deload (see file 10).
Repeat with modified exercise selection/rep ranges to manage staleness.
Drop sets: 1x/exercise/week on a final set of isolation work.
Rest-pause: On plateaued isolation lifts to add density without adding time.
Supersets: Antagonist pairing (biceps/triceps, chest/back) to increase session efficiency once a 4-5 day split is time-constrained.
Beginners: mostly machine/DB-based, simple patterns.

Intermediates: introduce more free-weight variation, unilateral work, and weak-point-specific accessories (e.g., pause squats for a sticking point, deficit deadlifts for pull off the floor, incline press for upper chest lag).
Rotate 1-2 accessory exercises every mesocycle (4-6 weeks) to manage staleness while keeping main compounds stable for trackable progression.
Deload frequency: every 5-8 weeks depending on intensity of preceding block.
Monitor: bar speed on submaximal sets, sleep quality, motivation, joint niggles — see file 10 for full fatigue-indicator table.
Female intermediates often tolerate the higher end of volume ranges sooner; consider starting at MAV rather than MEV for lower body work if recovery markers are good.
30-40 age group: still full capacity, but should get more deliberate about warm-ups and joint-prep for high-volume blocks.
40-50: consider capping frequency at 5 days, longer deload cycles if fatigue accumulates faster than in earlier training years.
Staying on linear progression far past the point of diminishing returns instead of transitioning to double progression/DUP.
Adding too many intensity techniques at once, burying recovery capacity.
Never rotating exercise selection, leading to joint wear and staleness.
Chasing every new "advanced" technique seen online without a clear volume/fatigue rationale.
"Intermediates need constant program-hopping to keep progressing." — False; consistent mesocycles with planned variation outperform constant switching.
"You need max effort singles regularly to get stronger." — False for hypertrophy/powerbuilding goals; submaximal, higher-volume work drives most of the adaptation.
5_Advanced_Programming.md: Progress is measured in months/years, not weeks.
High accuracy in RIR/RPE self-assessment.
High volume tolerance but also high fatigue accumulation risk — MRV ceiling becomes a real, frequently-bumped constraint.
Often has old injuries/joint wear requiring permanent exercise modifications.
Responds well to specialization blocks and requires genuine periodization (not just "add weight when possible").
PPL x2 (6-day) — most common for bodybuilding-focused advanced lifters.
Bro Split — viable ONLY once MAV is already being met via other structures and per-session specialization is desired.
Squat/Bench/Deadlift 4-day or Conjugate — for strength/powerlifting-focused advanced lifters.

**Definition:** A 4-8 week block where 1-2 lagging muscle groups receive volume above their normal MAV (temporarily pushing toward MRV) while all other muscle groups are dropped to MEV (maintenance) volume.
**Example — Shoulder specialization block:**
Shoulders: 20-24 sets/week (temporary MRV push)
All other muscles: MEV maintenance only (e.g., chest 6-8 sets, back 8-10 sets)
**Rule:** Never specialize more than 2 muscle groups simultaneously — total systemic fatigue budget doesn't allow it.
**Post-block:** Return all muscles to normal MEV-MAV distribution; the specialized muscle's MEV often "resets" slightly higher (a trained adaptation), allowing a new normal baseline going forward.
Standard for powerlifters approaching a meet.
Hypertrophy block (4-6 wks, higher volume, 65-75% intensity) → Strength block (3-5 wks, 75-85%, lower volume) → Peaking block (2-3 wks, 85-95%+, very low volume) → Taper/Meet.
Max Effort days rotate exercise variation weekly (e.g., box squat, safety bar squat, front squat) worked up to a heavy single/triple.
Dynamic Effort days use submaximal loads (50-60%) with maximal bar speed for multiple sets of 2-3 reps (often with bands/chains for accommodating resistance).
Best for lifters who've plateaued on block/linear models and need constant novel max-effort stimulus.
Multiple quality-days per week per lift (e.g., strength day, hypertrophy day, technique/speed day) with tightly controlled volume landmarks per day.
Myo-reps, rest-pause, drop sets, mechanical drop sets, cluster sets, forced reps, partials, BFR/occlusion work on isolation exercises, eccentric overload.

**Rule:** Deploy intensity techniques as fatigue-efficient volume additions in the final 1-2 weeks of a mesocycle (when adding full extra sets is no longer recoverable) rather than throughout the whole block.
"Muscle memory" (myonuclei retention + neural re-learning) allows faster-than-novice re-progression.

Protocol: Start at roughly 60-70% of previous peak volume and intensity for 2 weeks, then ramp aggressively over the following 2-4 weeks back toward prior levels, monitoring joint feedback closely (connective tissue detrains/retrains slower than muscle).

**Natural lifters:** MRV ceiling is a hard, real constraint; recovery between high-volume blocks is essential; specialization blocks must be time-limited (4-8 weeks) with a return to maintenance elsewhere.

**Enhanced athletes (only where directly relevant, e.g., adapting a program requested for an already-enhanced client):** Recovery capacity and MRV ceiling are substantially elevated, allowing higher frequency, volume, and reduced deload need. The AI should not assume enhancement status and should default to natural-lifter fatigue management rules unless explicitly told otherwise.
Deload frequency: every 4-6 weeks during high-volume/high-intensity blocks (more frequent than intermediates due to closer proximity to MRV).

Sleep, protein, and stress management become higher-leverage (and higher-stakes) than at any other training age — small recovery deficits meaningfully blunt an advanced lifter's much narrower gains window.
Use posing/visual assessment (or client-reported photos) to identify genuinely lagging muscle groups vs. overall proportion issues.

Apply specialization blocks (Section 3) to address genuine lags; avoid "specializing" a muscle that is merely less visually dominant due to genetics/insertion length, which volume cannot fully correct.
Running permanent MRV-level volume across all muscles simultaneously — unsustainable and leads to overreaching.
Never deloading because "progress feels good."
Copying a pro bodybuilder's program/volume without matching recovery capacity or (often) pharmacological support.
Treating every plateau as a programming problem rather than checking recovery/nutrition first.
"Advanced lifters need constant max-effort work to keep progressing." — False; most advanced hypertrophy progress still comes from submaximal, high-quality volume.
"If a natural lifter trains like a pro bodybuilder, they'll get similar results." — False; volume tolerance and recovery capacity differ substantially without pharmacological support.
6_Intensity_Techniques.md: **Protocol:** Perform a set to near failure (0-1 RIR), immediately reduce load 20-30%, continue to near failure again. Can repeat for 2-3 total drops.
**Best exercises:** Machines, cables, DB isolation (leg extension, lateral raise, leg press, pulldown).
**Avoid on:** Free-weight compounds requiring technical precision under fatigue (barbell squat, deadlift) — safety risk.
**Who:** Intermediate+ (6+ months), used 1x/exercise/week max on a final set.
**Fatigue cost:** Moderate-high; counts as roughly 1.5-2x a normal set for fatigue-budget purposes.
**Protocol:** Move through 2-3 exercise variations of decreasing mechanical difficulty for the same muscle without rest (e.g., Incline DB Press → Flat DB Press → DB Floor Press).
**Best for:** Chest, shoulders, quads — muscle groups with clear leverage-based exercise variants.
**Who:** Intermediate-advanced.
**Protocol:** Perform a set to near failure, rest 15-20 seconds, perform additional reps, repeat rest-pause 1-2 more times.
**Best exercises:** Machines, DB work, bodyweight movements.
**Who:** Intermediate+; excellent for time-constrained sessions (adds volume without adding a full extra set + full rest).
**Protocol:** One "activation set" of ~12-20 reps to near failure, then rest 20-30 seconds, perform mini-sets of 3-5 reps repeated 3-5 times with short rests, stopping when reps drop below 3.
**Best for:** Isolation exercises, high-frequency specialization phases.
**Who:** Intermediate-advanced; efficient volume accumulation tool.
**Protocol:** Break a heavy set into smaller clusters with short intra-set rest (e.g., 5x2 with 15-20s rest between pairs = effectively a 10-rep set at higher-than-normal load).
**Best for:** Strength/power work on main compound lifts.
**Who:** Intermediate-advanced strength/powerlifting-focused clients.
**Protocol:** Spotter assists with minimal force to complete 1-3 reps beyond failure.
**Who:** Advanced only; requires a competent, trusted spotter; use sparingly (1x/exercise/week max) due to very high fatigue/injury cost.
**Protocol:** Deliberate, controlled use of momentum (e.g., slight body English on a barbell curl) to complete extra reps once strict form fails, then control the eccentric.
**Who:** Advanced only, and only on exercises where the loss of strict form doesn't create injury risk (avoid on lower back-loaded movements).
**Protocol:** After reaching failure in full range, continue with partial-range reps in the strongest portion of the movement.
**Best for:** Extending a set's time-under-tension/fatigue on isolation exercises.

**Protocol:** Apply moderate occlusion (via BFR cuffs/bands, ~40-80% arterial occlusion pressure by feel/device) to a limb, then perform very light load (20-30% 1RM) for high reps (15-30) with short rest, 3-4 sets.
**Best for:** Rehab/injury contexts where heavy loading is contraindicated, or as an accessory tool for arms/legs to add metabolic stress without heavy joint loading.
**Contraindications:** Cardiovascular conditions, blood clotting disorders, pregnancy — flag for medical clearance if any of these are present or unknown.
**Who:** Any training age in a rehab context (physician-guided); intermediate-advanced in a performance-enhancement context.
**Protocol:** Load 105-120% of concentric 1RM, control a slow (3-5s) eccentric, use spotters/machine to reset the concentric (or a partner-assisted lift back to top).
**Best for:** Advanced strength/power athletes, tendon-loading phases, breaking through strength plateaus.
**Risk:** High; requires excellent technique and either a machine or highly competent spotters.
**Protocol:** Hold a position under tension (e.g., mid-range squat hold, pause at sticking point) for 5-15+ seconds.
**Best for:** Sticking-point-specific strength work, tendon health, rehab, and building positional strength/confidence.
**Protocol:** Submaximal loads (30-60%) moved with maximal intended bar speed; or plyometric variations (box jumps, med ball throws) for bodyweight-based power.
**Best for:** Athletic performance goals, dynamic effort days in conjugate programming.
**Who:** Intermediate-advanced with sound technique base; not for true beginners due to technical demand under speed.
Using drop sets/forced reps on every set of every session — this guarantees excess fatigue and stalled progress.
Applying advanced techniques to beginners "for intensity" without adequate volume tolerance built first.
Using free-weight compound lifts (squat, deadlift) for forced reps or heavy eccentric overload without proper spotting/safety setup.
Treating intensity techniques as a substitute for insufficient baseline weekly volume.
"More intensity techniques always means more growth." — False; fatigue cost scales, often faster than the added stimulus.
"Drop sets are necessary for muscle definition." — False; they're one efficient tool among many, not a requirement.
7_Goal_Based_Modifications.md: **Lean Bulk (recommended default for most clients):** Small surplus (~250-300 kcal above maintenance), gain ~0.25-0.5% bodyweight/week; minimizes fat gain while supporting muscle growth; ideal for clients who care about aesthetics year-round.

**Dirty Bulk:** Large surplus, faster weight/strength gain, more fat gain — appropriate for underweight clients needing to add mass quickly, or strength athletes prioritizing absolute weight class/leverage over leanness. Coach note: warn client explicitly about the fat-gain tradeoff and plan a subsequent cut.
**Rate-of-gain check:** >0.5-1% bodyweight/week gain in a natural lifter signals excess fat gain relative to muscle — reduce surplus.
**Training implication:** Both use standard hypertrophy programming (see files 3/4/5); surplus size does not change set/rep prescriptions, only recovery capacity and gain rate.
**Strength (general):** Bias main lifts to 1-6 rep range, moderate volume, longer rest, prioritize technique/skill practice on competition-relevant lifts (or main lifts of interest).
**Powerlifting:** Periodize toward a meet using block periodization (see file 5) — hypertrophy block → strength block → peaking block → taper. Reduce accessory volume as competition nears.

**Powerbuilding:** Blend — heavy compound work (3-6 reps) for 2-3 main lifts/week, plus genuine hypertrophy accessory volume (8-15 reps) for aesthetics. Most versatile "best of both worlds" goal, well suited to Upper/Lower or PPL splits.
**Off-season:** Surplus, higher volume (MAV-approaching-MRV), rep ranges 6-15, full intensity technique toolkit available (advanced lifters).

**Contest Prep:** Progressive deficit, cardio ramps up, training volume generally maintained as long as possible (drop only if recovery genuinely fails) to preserve muscle in a deficit; posing practice added; final 1-2 weeks (peak week) involves manipulation of water/sodium/carbs — highly individual, requires experienced coaching oversight, general AI guidance should stay conservative here and recommend a specialized coach for peak week specifics.

**Coach note:** The biggest bodybuilding-prep mistake is cutting training volume too early to "save energy" — volume should be the last thing reduced, after cardio and further calorie reduction, since it's the primary muscle-preservation signal.
**Off-season:** Hypertrophy + strength base building, higher lifting volume, power work introduced late off-season.
**Pre-season:** Strength-to-power conversion, reduce volume, increase intensity and explosive work.

**In-season:** Maintenance lifting only (40-60% volume reduction from off-season peak), 2x/week max typically, prioritize recovery for sport performance over lifting gains; heavy compound work retained at lower volume to preserve strength (research supports maintaining intensity while cutting volume in-season).
**Coach note:** Never program a new hypertrophy specialization block during a competitive season — recovery capacity must be reserved for sport demands.

**Deficit sizing:** 300-500 kcal for most; up to 750 kcal for higher body-fat clients able to tolerate it; smaller (~10-15%) deficits as client approaches leaner body-fat levels to protect muscle and hormonal health.

**Training:** Maintain resistance training volume/intensity as close to muscle-building levels as possible — this is the #1 muscle-preservation lever in a deficit. Do not reduce weights/reps preemptively; only reduce if genuine recovery failure occurs.

**Cardio:** Add progressively (start with NEAT/step count increases before formal cardio); use cardio as the primary lever for increasing deficit rather than crashing calories further, which protects diet adherence and muscle retention.
**Refeeds/diet breaks:** For deficits lasting >8-12 weeks, schedule a 1-2 week maintenance "diet break" to restore hormones (leptin, thyroid markers) and psychological adherence.
**Common mistake:** Cutting resistance training volume alongside calories — this compounds muscle loss risk instead of protecting against it.

Maintain current volume/intensity at MEV-to-low-MAV; primary goal is sustainability, not progression — some progression can still occur (especially for less advanced trainees) but is not the driving focus.
Good default goal for high-stress life periods, travel, or "off-phase" between dedicated bulk/cut blocks.

**Who it works best for:** Beginners, detrained returning lifters, clients with higher body fat starting a resistance program, or anyone reintroducing training after a long layoff — these populations can build muscle and lose fat simultaneously.

**Who it does NOT work well for:** Advanced, already-lean natural lifters — simultaneous recomposition becomes very slow/impractical at this stage; sequential bulk/cut phases are more effective and should be recommended instead.

**Nutrition:** Maintenance calories or very small deficit (5-10%), protein pushed to the higher end (2.2-2.6g/kg) to maximize the muscle-protein-synthesis signal while in energy balance/slight deficit.

Full-body 2-4x/week, moderate volume (MEV range), balance of strength/hypertrophy/cardio work; the goal is broad health/functional capacity, not maximal adaptation in any one quality — programming should be simple, sustainable, and enjoyable to maximize long-term adherence.
Reducing training volume as the first lever in a fat-loss phase (should be the last lever).
Recommending aggressive recomposition to advanced lean lifters where it's biologically slow/impractical.
Ignoring in-season vs off-season demands for athletes and programming identically year-round.
Applying the same deficit size regardless of starting body-fat level.
"You can't build muscle in a deficit." — False for beginners, detrained, and higher-body-fat populations; true mainly for lean advanced natural lifters.
"Bulking requires eating as much as possible." — False; excessive surplus mostly adds fat, not muscle, beyond a natural gain-rate ceiling.

"Cardio kills gains." — False in reasonable doses; excessive, poorly-recovered cardio volume can interfere, but moderate cardio supports fat loss and cardiovascular health without meaningfully blunting hypertrophy.
8_Weekly_Muscle_Volume.md: **MEV (Minimum Effective Volume):** The lowest weekly set count that still produces measurable progress. Used as a floor during deloads, high-fatigue periods, and for non-specialized muscles during a specialization block.
**MAV (Maximum Adaptive Volume):** The range producing the best progress-to-fatigue-cost ratio for most people, most of the time. The default target range for a normal training block.

**MRV (Maximum Recoverable Volume):** The ceiling beyond which additional volume no longer helps (or actively harms) recovery and progress. Only approached temporarily (specialization blocks) and only by intermediate-advanced trainees with excellent recovery.
Applying advanced MRV numbers to beginners/intermediates.
Ignoring indirect volume contributions, leading to accidental overshooting of MRV for triceps/shoulders from heavy pressing-focused programs.
Treating these numbers as rigid rather than starting points requiring individual feedback-based adjustment.
Increasing volume as the automatic fix for every plateau without first checking recovery/nutrition (see file 2, plateau tree).
"More sets is always better." — False beyond MRV; performance and recovery worsen.
"Everyone has the same MEV/MAV/MRV." — False; individual variation is large; genetics, training age, recovery capacity, and even exercise selection (SFR) all shift these numbers.
13_Cardio_and_Conditioning_Programming.m: Prescribing HIIT to yellow/orange-tier or medically-flagged clients.
Ignoring interference effect for strength/hypertrophy-primary clients by over-prescribing cardio.
Using HR-based zones for clients on heart-rate-altering medications.
"Cardio kills gains" — only true at excessive volumes without Section 4 management; moderate Zone 2 cardio does not meaningfully impair hypertrophy/strength when properly sequenced.
"Fasted cardio burns more fat long-term" — total energy balance over time drives fat loss more than fasted-state timing.
14_Default_Safe_Template_and_Coach_Overr: No exercises flagged `pain_provoking_movement` for that individual (file 11 Section 4).
No barbell max-effort or near-max-effort work.
No unsupervised overhead loaded work if `thoracic_mobility_limited` flag present — substitute landmine or banded pattern.
No numeric calorie/macro targets if any ED flag present (file 12 Section 6) — nutrition guidance limited to general qualitative habits (protein-forward meals, whole-food emphasis, hydration).
No body-composition tracking prompts if ED flag present.
Every override is immutable once logged (append-only; corrections are new entries referencing the original, not edits).
Weekly automated report of all overrides surfaced to gym owner/admin account.
Any override touching a client with an active file-12 flag is auto-flagged for priority admin review.
Client-facing app shows "your trainer adjusted this" transparency note where appropriate, without exposing raw audit data to the client.
